title: Hernias: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Identify key diagnostic features of Hernias
Apply clinical reasoning to Hernias management

sections.title: Overview of Hernias
sections.content:
**Hernias**



**Key Points:**
• Indirect inguinal: through internal ring, lateral to inferior epigastric vessels, follows spermatic cord (most common hernia)
• Direct inguinal: through Hesselbach triangle (floor of inguinal canal), medial to inferior epigastric vessels
• Femoral: below inguinal ligament, medial to femoral vein; highest risk of incarceration/strangulation

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Indirect inguinal: through internal ring, lateral to inferior epigastric vessels, follows spermatic cord (most common hernia)
• Direct inguinal: through Hesselbach triangle (floor of inguinal canal), medial to inferior epigastric vessels

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Femoral: below inguinal ligament, medial to femoral vein; highest risk of incarceration/strangulation
• Incarcerated: cannot be reduced (emergency if bowel obstruction); Strangulated: compromised blood supply (surgical emergency)
• Hesselbach triangle: inferior epigastric vessels (lateral), rectus abdominis (medial), inguinal ligament (inferior)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Hernias:**

• Femoral: below inguinal ligament, medial to femoral vein; highest risk of incarceration/strangulation
• Incarcerated: cannot be reduced (emergency if bowel obstruction); Strangulated: compromised blood supply (surgical emergency)
• Hesselbach triangle: inferior epigastric vessels (lateral), rectus abdominis (medial), inguinal ligament (inferior)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Hernias?

options.text: Indirect inguinal: through internal ring, lateral to inferior epigastric vessels, follows spermatic cord (most common hernia)
options.explanation: Indirect inguinal: through internal ring, lateral to inferior epigastric vessels, follows spermatic cord (most common hernia)

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Hernias?

options.text: Hesselbach triangle: inferior epigastric vessels (lateral), rectus abdominis (medial), inguinal ligament (inferior)
options.explanation: Hesselbach triangle: inferior epigastric vessels (lateral), rectus abdominis (medial), inguinal ligament (inferior)

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Hernias. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Direct inguinal: through Hesselbach triangle (floor of inguinal canal), medial to inferior epigastric vessels
options.explanation: Direct inguinal: through Hesselbach triangle (floor of inguinal canal), medial to inferior epigastric vessels

mcqs.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.
While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.
